title: Argyrol on Cotton — Silver Proteinate Ion Reduction
stainChemistry: Argyrol is a colloidal silver proteinate solution (silver ions complexed with protein colloids) used historically as an eye drops and topical antiseptic. When argyrol contacts cotton, the silver ions (Ag⁺) form extremely stable complexes with cellulose hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups. The silver ions are reduced by atmospheric oxygen and organic compounds in the fiber, forming insoluble silver compounds (Ag₂O, metallic Ag) that appear as brown, gray, or black stains. The stain is essentially fixed silver oxide — highly resistant to simple washing.
whyThisWorks: Silver ion stains respond to sodium thiosulfate, which forms soluble silver thiosulfate complexes: Ag⁺ + 2S₂O₃²⁻ → [Ag(S₂O₃)₂]³⁻. The complex is soluble and washes away. Hydrogen peroxide can help oxidize any remaining brown silver oxide residues. The key is understanding that this is a metallic salt stain, not a dye — different chemistry, different approach.

spottingProtocol.agent: sodium thiosulfate solution (1 oz hypo per 16 oz water, tepid)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge the thiosulfate solution into the stain from the back side. Work from outside the stain inward. The brown or gray argyrol stain will gradually fade as the silver ions form soluble complexes. Keep the area moist and sponge repeatedly. The color change may be gradual — be patient.

spottingProtocol.agent: hydrogen peroxide 3% (only if brown residue remains)
spottingProtocol.instruction: If brown silver oxide residue persists after thiosulfate treatment, apply hydrogen peroxide 3% to oxidize and bleach the remaining brown compounds. Work gently. This is a secondary step — many argyrol stains will fully respond to thiosulfate alone.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse extensively with distilled water. Silver thiosulfate complexes are soluble and will wash away with water. Perform 3-4 complete rinses, replacing the under-pad between each. Thorough rinsing prevents re-oxidation of silver ions.

spottingProtocol.agent: 5% acetic acid solution (white vinegar 1:3 with water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final sponge with acetic acid to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and prevent post-spotting re-oxidation of silver ions. The acid keeps silver in solution if any trace ions remain.

escalation: If argyrol stain persists as a dark brown or black mark after the full protocol, the silver may have oxidized to insoluble silver oxide (Ag₂O) or even metallic silver, which sodium thiosulfate cannot dissolve. This occurs with old, set-in stains. Do not repeat — the stain is likely permanent. Professional treatment with thiourea (which dissolves metallic and oxide silver) is the only option, but this is harsh and carries fiber damage risk. For very old argyrol stains, the stain may be permanent.
commonMistakes: Using chlorine bleach instead of thiosulfate — chlorine reacts with silver to form black silver chloride (AgCl), making the stain darker and more visible.
Not rinsing thoroughly — residual silver ions in the fiber will re-oxidize to brown within days.
Using hot water — heat accelerates silver oxidation and can set the stain deeper.
scienceNote: The argyrol stain is a practical lesson in metallic salt chemistry on textiles. Most spotters think of stains as dyes or organic compounds, but argyrol (and similar silver-containing stains from electrical contacts, old jewelry, etc.) are inorganic metallic salts. The thiosulfate complexation mechanism is the same chemistry used in silver recovery from photographic waste — this knowledge transfer shows the value of understanding chemistry fundamentally, not just memorizing protocols.
safety: Sodium thiosulfate is non-toxic. Hydrogen peroxide 3% is safe. NEVER use chlorine bleach on silver-containing stains — it forms black silver chloride. Standard spotting gloves recommended. Test all chemicals on a seam first. No special ventilation required.
materialWarnings: Cotton is chemically stable with all reagents.
However, if the argyrol stain is very old and has oxidized to black metallic silver or silver oxide, attempting to remove it with thiourea (stronger treatment) can weaken cotton fibers.
Air dry only after spotting — avoid heat.
callPro: For valuable cotton garments with old or dark argyrol stains, professional treatment is safer. If the stain is black and has been present for years, it may be permanent regardless of treatment method. Professional may recommend acceptance of the stain rather than risking fiber damage.
customerExplanation: Argyrol is a silver-containing compound that forms insoluble silver oxide stains on cotton. We used sodium thiosulfate, which forms soluble complexes with silver ions, allowing them to wash away. For any remaining brown residue, we applied hydrogen peroxide to complete the bleaching. The garment was thoroughly rinsed and neutralized to prevent re-oxidation.

homeSolutions.agent: This is a metallic-salt stain — do NOT reach for bleach
homeSolutions.instruction: Argyrol is a silver-containing antiseptic (old medical eye drops, some topical disinfectants). The stain is silver ions bonded into the cotton. Critical rule: NEVER use chlorine bleach on silver — it reacts to form black silver chloride, making the stain DARKER and more permanent. Also never hot water — heat accelerates silver oxidation and sets the stain. You have one real home option: sodium thiosulfate (photo "hypo" or pool chlorine neutralizer), which dissolves silver ions into a washable complex.

homeSolutions.agent: Get sodium thiosulfate — it's the home tool that matters
homeSolutions.instruction: Sodium thiosulfate is sold as "hypo" in photography stores (darkroom supply) and as "chlorine neutralizer" at pool-supply stores. A small bottle is under $10 and solves this stain. Without it, no amount of vinegar or dish soap will do more than slightly fade the stain. Realistic home outcome without thiosulfate: minimal. With thiosulfate: most fresh stains lift cleanly.

Dab a drop of your thiosulfate solution on an inside seam. Wait 60 seconds, blot with a white cloth. If the cotton holds its color, proceed. Then turn the garment inside out and flush the stain with cold water from the back for 30 seconds — removes any loose silver before chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Sodium thiosulfate solution — the whole game
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon of sodium thiosulfate crystals in 1 cup of cool (not warm) water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, press on the stain — outside-in. Keep the area moist by re-wetting every 2–3 minutes. Let dwell 8–10 minutes total. You'll see the brown or gray fade gradually as the thiosulfate forms soluble silver-thiosulfate complexes. Blot between cycles with a fresh cloth.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix sodium thiosulfate with chlorine bleach, acid, or hydrogen peroxide in the same dwell. Work in a ventilated area.

homeSolutions.instruction: After thiosulfate, if a brown trace remains, that's silver oxide. Dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a clean white cloth and press onto the spot for 3–5 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes the residual brown compounds. Blot, then move to the rinse step. Skip this step on colored cotton — test first on the patch. Rinse any thiosulfate out fully before applying peroxide.

Rinse the area thoroughly with cold distilled water (tap water has minerals that can react with residual silver). 3–4 rinses minimum. Any trace of silver thiosulfate left in the fiber will re-oxidize to brown within days if not removed. Finish with a vinegar rinse (1 tbsp white vinegar in 1 cup cold water) for 1 minute to prevent re-oxidation and restore fiber pH. Air dry — no heat.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for old, dark, or heat-set argyrol
homeSolutions.instruction: If the argyrol is years old, black, or was already washed in hot water before you saw it, the silver has oxidized past what thiosulfate can dissolve. A pro may try thiourea (stronger silver complex-former) but it carries fiber-damage risk. Tell them: "Argyrol (silver proteinate) on cotton, age [x], treated at home with sodium thiosulfate + 3% peroxide. No chlorine bleach used (confirmed). Residual black/brown remains." Realistic: very old argyrol stains are often permanent even with professional treatment.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Sodium Thiosulfate (Hypo Crystals)
products.note: Essential for silver ion complexation. Same product as for iodine stains, but here it's dissolving silver, not reducing iodine. Different chemistry, same reagent.

products.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% USP
products.note: For residual brown silver oxide. Use only if thiosulfate alone doesn't fully remove the stain.

aftercare: Apply a light dusting of sodium thiosulfate powder to the argyrol stain. Work gently into the cotton fibers. The powder will begin to form soluble complexes with silver ions on the fiber surface. This is the preliminary step — the powder won't fully dissolve the stain, but prepares it for wet treatment.
